question: 2. If a polynomial \( f(x) \) is divided by \( (x-a)^{2}(x-b) \), where \( a \neq b \), derive a formula for the remainder.
solution:
First Solution. Since \( f(x) \) is divided by a cubic polynomial, the remainder \( R(x) \) will be of degree at most two in \( x \), say \( R(x)=A x^{2}+B x+C \). Then
\[
f(x)=(x-a)^{2}(x-b) Q(x)+A x^{2}+B x+C
\]
and
\[
\begin{array}{l}
f^{\prime}(x)=2(x-a)(x-b) Q(x)+(x-a)^{2} Q(x) \\
\quad+(x-a)^{2}(x-b) Q^{\prime}(x)+2 A x+B .
\end{array}
\]

From these relations one gets
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(a) & =A a^{2}+B a+C \\
f(b) & =A b^{2}+B b+C \\
f^{\prime}(a) & =2 A a+B
\end{aligned}
\]

Solving for \( A, B, C \) one gets
\[
\begin{array}{l}
A=\frac{1}{(b-a)^{2}}\left[f(b)-f(a)-(b-a) f^{\prime}(a)\right] \\
B=\frac{-1}{(b-a)^{2}}\left[2 a(f(b)-f(a))-\left(b^{2}-a^{2}\right) f^{\prime}(a)\right] \\
C=\frac{1}{(b-a)^{2}}\left[(b-a)^{2} f(a)+a^{2}(f(b)-f(a))+a b(a-b) f^{\prime}(a)\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
R(x)=\frac{1}{(b-a)^{2}} & \left\{\left(f(b)-f(a)-(b-a) f^{\prime}(a)\right) x^{2}\right. \\
& -\left(2 a(f(b)-f(a))+\left(b^{2}-a^{2}\right) f^{\prime}(a)\right) x \\
& \left.+\left((b-a)^{2} f(a)+a^{2}(f(b)-f(a))+a b(a-b) f^{\prime}(a)\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

This is easier to check if written in the form
\[
R(x)=f(a)+\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{(b-a)^{2}}(x-a)^{2}-\frac{f^{\prime}(a)}{b-a}(x-a)(x-b) .
\]

Second Solution. We can write
\[
f(x)=f(a)+(x-a) f^{\prime}(a)+(x-a)^{2} g(x)
\]
where \( g \) is a polynomial, and \( g(x)=g(b)+(x-b) h(x) \) where \( h \) is a polynomial. Then
\[
f(x)=f(a)+(x-a) f^{\prime}(a)+(x-a)^{2} g(b)+(x-a)^{2}(x-b) h(x)
\]
and the desired remainder is
\[
f(a)+(x-a) f^{\prime}(a)+(x-a)^{2} g(b)
\]

Substituting \( b \) for \( x \) in (1) gives us \( g(b) \) and the remainder is
\[
f(a)+(x-a) f^{\prime}(a)+\frac{(x-a)^{2}}{(b-a)^{2}}\left(f(b)-f(a)-(b-a) f^{\prime}(a)\right) .
\]

descriptive_long.question: If a polynomial \( polyfunc(variable) \) is divided by \( (variable-firstconst)^{2}(variable-secondconst) \), where \( firstconst \neq secondconst \), derive a formula for the remainder.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Since \( polyfunc(variable) \) is divided by a cubic polynomial, the remainder \( remainder(variable) \) will be of degree at most two in \( variable \), say \( remainder(variable)=coefquadr\, variable^{2}+coeflinear\, variable+coefconst \). Then
\[
polyfunc(variable)=(variable-firstconst)^{2}(variable-secondconst)\, quotient(variable)+coefquadr\, variable^{2}+coeflinear\, variable+coefconst
\]
and
\[
\begin{array}{l}
polyfunc^{\prime}(variable)=2(variable-firstconst)(variable-secondconst)\, quotient(variable)+(variable-firstconst)^{2}\, quotient(variable) \\
\quad+(variable-firstconst)^{2}(variable-secondconst)\, quotient^{\prime}(variable)+2\, coefquadr\, variable+coeflinear .
\end{array}
\]

From these relations one gets
\[
\begin{aligned}
polyfunc(firstconst) & =coefquadr\, firstconst^{2}+coeflinear\, firstconst+coefconst \\
polyfunc(secondconst) & =coefquadr\, secondconst^{2}+coeflinear\, secondconst+coefconst \\
polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst) & =2\, coefquadr\, firstconst+coeflinear
\end{aligned}
\]

Solving for \( coefquadr, coeflinear, coefconst \) one gets
\[
\begin{array}{l}
coefquadr=\dfrac{1}{(secondconst-firstconst)^{2}}\left[polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)-(secondconst-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right] \\
coeflinear=\dfrac{-1}{(secondconst-firstconst)^{2}}\left[2\, firstconst\left(polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)\right)-\left(secondconst^{2}-firstconst^{2}\right) polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right] \\
coefconst=\dfrac{1}{(secondconst-firstconst)^{2}}\left[(secondconst-firstconst)^{2} polyfunc(firstconst)+firstconst^{2}\left(polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)\right)+firstconst\, secondconst(firstconst-secondconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
remainder(variable)=\dfrac{1}{(secondconst-firstconst)^{2}} & \left\{\left(polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)-(secondconst-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right) variable^{2}\right. \\
& -\left(2\, firstconst\left(polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)\right)+\left(secondconst^{2}-firstconst^{2}\right) polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right) variable \\
& \left.+\left((secondconst-firstconst)^{2} polyfunc(firstconst)+firstconst^{2}\left(polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)\right)+firstconst\, secondconst(firstconst-secondconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

This is easier to check if written in the form
\[
remainder(variable)=polyfunc(firstconst)+\frac{polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)}{(secondconst-firstconst)^{2}}(variable-firstconst)^{2}-\frac{polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)}{secondconst-firstconst}(variable-firstconst)(variable-secondconst) .
\]

Second Solution. We can write
\[
polyfunc(variable)=polyfunc(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)^{2}\, auxpoly(variable)
\]
where \( auxpoly \) is a polynomial, and \( auxpoly(variable)=auxpoly(secondconst)+(variable-secondconst)\, helperpoly(variable) \) where \( helperpoly \) is a polynomial. Then
\[
polyfunc(variable)=polyfunc(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)^{2}\, auxpoly(secondconst)+(variable-firstconst)^{2}(variable-secondconst)\, helperpoly(variable)
\]
and the desired remainder is
\[
polyfunc(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)^{2}\, auxpoly(secondconst)
\]

Substituting \( secondconst \) for \( variable \) in (1) gives us \( auxpoly(secondconst) \) and the remainder is
\[
polyfunc(firstconst)+(variable-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)+\frac{(variable-firstconst)^{2}}{(secondconst-firstconst)^{2}}\left(polyfunc(secondconst)-polyfunc(firstconst)-(secondconst-firstconst)\, polyfunc^{\prime}(firstconst)\right) .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. If a polynomial \( rhinoceros(grapefruit) \) is divided by \( (grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2}(grapefruit-butterscotch) \), where \( lemongrass \neq butterscotch \), derive a formula for the remainder.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Since \( rhinoceros(grapefruit) \) is divided by a cubic polynomial, the remainder \( cinematics(grapefruit) \) will be of degree at most two in grapefruit, say \( cinematics(grapefruit)=pineapples grapefruit^{2}+watermelon grapefruit+blacksmith \). Then
\[
rhinoceros(grapefruit)=(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2}(grapefruit-butterscotch) turpentine(grapefruit)+pineapples grapefruit^{2}+watermelon grapefruit+blacksmith
\]

and
\[
\begin{array}{l}
rhinoceros^{\prime}(grapefruit)=2(grapefruit-lemongrass)(grapefruit-butterscotch) turpentine(grapefruit)+(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2} turpentine(grapefruit) \\
\quad+(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2}(grapefruit-butterscotch) turpentine^{\prime}(grapefruit)+2 pineapples grapefruit+watermelon .
\end{array}
\]

From these relations one gets
\[
\begin{aligned}
rhinoceros(lemongrass) & =pineapples lemongrass^{2}+watermelon lemongrass+blacksmith \\
rhinoceros(butterscotch) & =pineapples butterscotch^{2}+watermelon butterscotch+blacksmith \\
rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass) & =2 pineapples lemongrass+watermelon
\end{aligned}
\]

Solving for \( pineapples, watermelon, blacksmith \) one gets
\[
\begin{array}{l}
pineapples=\frac{1}{(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2}}\left[rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass)-(butterscotch-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right] \\
watermelon=\frac{-1}{(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2}}\left[2 \,lemongrass\,(rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass))-\left(butterscotch^{2}-lemongrass^{2}\right) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right] \\
blacksmith=\frac{1}{(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2}}\left[(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2} rhinoceros(lemongrass)+lemongrass^{2}(rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass))+lemongrass butterscotch(lemongrass-butterscotch) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
cinematics(grapefruit)=\frac{1}{(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2}} & \left\{\left(rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass)-(butterscotch-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right) grapefruit^{2}\right. \\
& -\left(2 \, lemongrass\, (rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass))+\left(butterscotch^{2}-lemongrass^{2}\right) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right) grapefruit \\
& \left.+\left((butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2} rhinoceros(lemongrass)+lemongrass^{2}(rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass))+lemongrass butterscotch(lemongrass-butterscotch) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

This is easier to check if written in the form
\[
cinematics(grapefruit)=rhinoceros(lemongrass)+\frac{rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass)}{(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2}}(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2}-\frac{rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)}{butterscotch-lemongrass}(grapefruit-lemongrass)(grapefruit-butterscotch) .
\]

Second Solution. We can write
\[
rhinoceros(grapefruit)=rhinoceros(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2} parchment(grapefruit)
\]
where \( parchment \) is a polynomial, and \( parchment(grapefruit)=parchment(butterscotch)+(grapefruit-butterscotch) sugarcane(grapefruit) \) where \( sugarcane \) is a polynomial. Then
\[
rhinoceros(grapefruit)=rhinoceros(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2} parchment(butterscotch)+(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2}(grapefruit-butterscotch) sugarcane(grapefruit)
\]
and the desired remainder is
\[
rhinoceros(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2} parchment(butterscotch)
\]

Substituting \( butterscotch \) for \( grapefruit \) in (1) gives us \( parchment(butterscotch) \) and the remainder is
\[
rhinoceros(lemongrass)+(grapefruit-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)+\frac{(grapefruit-lemongrass)^{2}}{(butterscotch-lemongrass)^{2}}\left(rhinoceros(butterscotch)-rhinoceros(lemongrass)-(butterscotch-lemongrass) rhinoceros^{\prime}(lemongrass)\right) .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. If a polynomial \( nonfunction(constantvalue) \) is divided by \( (constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2}(constantvalue-firstconstant) \), where \( secondconstant \neq firstconstant \), derive a formula for the remainder.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. Since \( nonfunction(constantvalue) \) is divided by a cubic polynomial, the remainder \( quotientpart(constantvalue) \) will be of degree at most two in \( constantvalue \), say \( quotientpart(constantvalue)=zerovalue constantvalue^{2}+unityvalue constantvalue+variablevalue \). Then
\[
nonfunction(constantvalue)=(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2}(constantvalue-firstconstant) productpoly(constantvalue)+zerovalue constantvalue^{2}+unityvalue constantvalue+variablevalue
\]
and
\[
\begin{array}{l}
nonfunction^{\prime}(constantvalue)=2(constantvalue-secondconstant)(constantvalue-firstconstant) productpoly(constantvalue)+(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2} productpoly(constantvalue) \\
\quad+(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2}(constantvalue-firstconstant) productpoly^{\prime}(constantvalue)+2 zerovalue constantvalue+unityvalue .
\end{array}
\]

From these relations one gets
\[
\begin{aligned}
nonfunction(secondconstant) & =zerovalue secondconstant^{2}+unityvalue secondconstant+variablevalue \\
nonfunction(firstconstant) & =zerovalue firstconstant^{2}+unityvalue firstconstant+variablevalue \\
nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant) & =2 zerovalue secondconstant+unityvalue
\end{aligned}
\]

Solving for \( zerovalue, unityvalue, variablevalue \) one gets
\[
\begin{array}{l}
zerovalue=\frac{1}{(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2}}\left[nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant)-(firstconstant-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right] \\
unityvalue=\frac{-1}{(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2}}\left[2 secondconstant(nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant))-\left(firstconstant^{2}-secondconstant^{2}\right) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right] \\
variablevalue=\frac{1}{(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2}}\left[(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2} nonfunction(secondconstant)+secondconstant^{2}(nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant))+secondconstant firstconstant(secondconstant-firstconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
quotientpart(constantvalue)=\frac{1}{(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2}} & \left\{\left(nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant)-(firstconstant-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right) constantvalue^{2}\right. \\
& -\left(2 secondconstant(nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant))+\left(firstconstant^{2}-secondconstant^{2}\right) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right) constantvalue \\
& \left.+\left((firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2} nonfunction(secondconstant)+secondconstant^{2}(nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant))+secondconstant firstconstant(secondconstant-firstconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

This is easier to check if written in the form
\[
quotientpart(constantvalue)=nonfunction(secondconstant)+\frac{nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant)}{(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2}}(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2}-\frac{nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)}{firstconstant-secondconstant}(constantvalue-secondconstant)(constantvalue-firstconstant) .
\]

Second Solution. We can write
\[
nonfunction(constantvalue)=nonfunction(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2} flatfunction(constantvalue)
\]
where \( flatfunction \) is a polynomial, and \( flatfunction(constantvalue)=flatfunction(firstconstant)+(constantvalue-firstconstant) nonpolynomial(constantvalue) \) where \( nonpolynomial \) is a polynomial. Then
\[
nonfunction(constantvalue)=nonfunction(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2} flatfunction(firstconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2}(constantvalue-firstconstant) nonpolynomial(constantvalue)
\]
and the desired remainder is
\[
nonfunction(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2} flatfunction(firstconstant)
\]

Substituting \( firstconstant \) for \( constantvalue \) in (1) gives us \( flatfunction(firstconstant) \) and the remainder is
\[
nonfunction(secondconstant)+(constantvalue-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)+\frac{(constantvalue-secondconstant)^{2}}{(firstconstant-secondconstant)^{2}}\left(nonfunction(firstconstant)-nonfunction(secondconstant)-(firstconstant-secondconstant) nonfunction^{\prime}(secondconstant)\right) .
\]

garbled_string.question: 2. If a polynomial \( pceodtna(qzxwvtnp) \) is divided by \( (qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) \), where \( hjgrksla \neq mfldqzre \), derive a formula for the remainder.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. Since \( pceodtna(qzxwvtnp) \) is divided by a cubic polynomial, the remainder \( wqjznvbf(qzxwvtnp) \) will be of degree at most two in \( qzxwvtnp \), say \( wqjznvbf(qzxwvtnp)=lrxspjqd qzxwvtnp^{2}+ztmbqyla qzxwvtnp+ynskrejo \). Then
\[
pceodtna(qzxwvtnp)=(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) vgworhcu(qzxwvtnp)+lrxspjqd qzxwvtnp^{2}+ztmbqyla qzxwvtnp+ynskrejo
\]
and
\[
\begin{array}{l}
pceodtna^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)=2(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) vgworhcu(qzxwvtnp)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2} vgworhcu(qzxwvtnp) \\
\quad+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) vgworhcu^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)+2 lrxspjqd qzxwvtnp+ztmbqyla .
\end{array}
\]

From these relations one gets
\[
\begin{aligned}
pceodtna(hjgrksla) & = lrxspjqd hjgrksla^{2}+ztmbqyla hjgrksla+ynskrejo \\
pceodtna(mfldqzre) & = lrxspjqd mfldqzre^{2}+ztmbqyla mfldqzre+ynskrejo \\
pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla) & = 2 lrxspjqd hjgrksla+ztmbqyla
\end{aligned}
\]

Solving for \( lrxspjqd, ztmbqyla, ynskrejo \) one gets
\[
\begin{array}{l}
lrxspjqd=\dfrac{1}{(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2}}\left[pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)-(mfldqzre-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right] \\
ztmbqyla=\dfrac{-1}{(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2}}\left[2 hjgrksla\bigl(pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)\bigr)-\left(mfldqzre^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}\right) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right] \\
ynskrejo=\dfrac{1}{(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2}}\left[(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2} pceodtna(hjgrksla)+hjgrksla^{2}\bigl(pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)\bigr)+hjgrksla mfldqzre(hjgrksla-mfldqzre) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
wqjznvbf(qzxwvtnp)=\frac{1}{(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2}} & \left\{\left(pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)-(mfldqzre-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right) qzxwvtnp^{2}\right. \\
& -\left(2 hjgrksla\bigl(pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)\bigr)+\left(mfldqzre^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}\right) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right) qzxwvtnp \\
& \left.+\left((mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2} pceodtna(hjgrksla)+hjgrksla^{2}\bigl(pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)\bigr)+hjgrksla mfldqzre(hjgrksla-mfldqzre) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
\]

This is easier to check if written in the form
\[
wqjznvbf(qzxwvtnp)=pceodtna(hjgrksla)+\frac{pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)}{(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2}}(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}-\frac{pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)}{mfldqzre-hjgrksla}(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) .
\]

Second Solution. We can write
\[
pceodtna(qzxwvtnp)=pceodtna(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2} xsaqtoly(qzxwvtnp)
\]
where \( xsaqtoly \) is a polynomial, and \( xsaqtoly(qzxwvtnp)=xsaqtoly(mfldqzre)+(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) kdfgomur(qzxwvtnp) \) where \( kdfgomur \) is a polynomial. Then
\[
pceodtna(qzxwvtnp)=pceodtna(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2} xsaqtoly(mfldqzre)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}(qzxwvtnp-mfldqzre) kdfgomur(qzxwvtnp)
\]
and the desired remainder is
\[
pceodtna(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2} xsaqtoly(mfldqzre)
\]
Substituting \( mfldqzre \) for \( qzxwvtnp \) in (1) gives us \( xsaqtoly(mfldqzre) \) and the remainder is
\[
pceodtna(hjgrksla)+(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)+\frac{(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{2}}{(mfldqzre-hjgrksla)^{2}}\left(pceodtna(mfldqzre)-pceodtna(hjgrksla)-(mfldqzre-hjgrksla) pceodtna^{\prime}(hjgrksla)\right) .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let F be a field of characteristic 0 and let \alpha , \beta , \gamma  \in  F be three distinct scalars.  
For an arbitrary polynomial P(x) \in  F[x] divide it by  

  D(x)= (x-\alpha )^2\cdot (x-\beta )\cdot (x-\gamma )^2.  

Write  

  P(x)=D(x) Q(x)+R(x)  with deg R \leq  4.  

1.  Prove that R(x) is uniquely determined by the five Hermite conditions  

  R(\alpha )=P(\alpha ),  R'(\alpha )=P'(\alpha ),  
  R(\beta )=P(\beta ),  
  R(\gamma )=P(\gamma ),  R'(\gamma )=P'(\gamma ).  

2.  Obtain an explicit closed-form expression for R(x) that involves only  

  P(\alpha ), P'(\alpha ), P(\beta ), P(\gamma ), P'(\gamma )  

and the parameters \alpha , \beta , \gamma .  Your final answer must be a quartic polynomial whose coefficients are rational functions of \alpha , \beta , \gamma .
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
 \Delta _{\alpha \beta }=\beta -\alpha , \Delta _{\beta \gamma }=\gamma -\beta , \Delta _{\alpha \gamma }=\gamma -\alpha .  
All three numbers are non-zero because the nodes are distinct.

  
Step 1 - Dimension count and uniqueness  
D(x) has degree 5, hence the remainder R(x) must have degree \leq  4; the F-vector
space Q_4 of such polynomials is 5-dimensional.  
The five linear functionals  

 f\mapsto f(\alpha ), f\mapsto f'(\alpha ), f\mapsto f(\beta ), f\mapsto f(\gamma ), f\mapsto f'(\gamma )  

are linearly independent on Q_4, because each of them annihilates a different
hyperplane of codimension 1.  Therefore there is a unique quartic satisfying the
required Hermite data.

  
Step 2 - A quartic Newton-Hermite basis  
Choose the (upper-triangular) Newton basis  

 N_0(x)=1,  
 N_1(x)=x-\alpha ,  
 N_2(x)=(x-\alpha )^2,  
 N_3(x)=(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta ),  
 N_4(x)=(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta )(x-\gamma ).                     (1)

deg N_i=i, so every element of Q_4 is a unique linear combination

 R(x)=c_0N_0(x)+c_1N_1(x)+c_2N_2(x)+c_3N_3(x)+c_4N_4(x).     (2)

We determine the five coefficients c_0,\ldots ,c_4 from the Hermite data.

  
Step 3 - The first three coefficients  
(i) R(\alpha )=c_0=P(\alpha ) \Rightarrow  c_0=P(\alpha ).  
(ii) R'(\alpha )=c_1=P'(\alpha ) \Rightarrow  c_1=P'(\alpha ).

(iii) R(\beta )=P(\beta ) gives  
 P(\beta )=c_0+c_1\Delta _{\alpha \beta }+c_2\Delta _{\alpha \beta }^2  
so

 c_2 = [P(\beta )-P(\alpha )-\Delta _{\alpha \beta }P'(\alpha )] / \Delta _{\alpha \beta }^2.     (3)

  
Step 4 - The fourth coefficient  
Set x=\gamma  in (2):

 P(\gamma )=P(\alpha )+P'(\alpha )\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }+c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2 + c_3\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }.     (4)

Solving for c_3 yields  

 c_3 = [P(\gamma )-P(\alpha )-P'(\alpha )\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }-c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2]  
    / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }).     (4')

  
Step 5 - The last coefficient  
Differentiate (2).  Using

 N_0'=0, N_1'=1, N_2'=2(x-\alpha ),  
 N_3'=2(x-\alpha )(x-\beta )+(x-\alpha )^2,  
 N_4'=2(x-\alpha )(x-\beta )(x-\gamma )+(x-\alpha )^2(x-\gamma )+(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta ),

evaluation at x=\gamma  gives  

 R'(\gamma )=P'(\alpha )+2c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }+c_3[2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }\Delta _{\beta \gamma }+\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2]+c_4\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }.     (5)

Because R'(\gamma )=P'(\gamma ), we solve for c_4:

 c_4 = [ P'(\gamma ) - P'(\alpha ) - 2c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma } - c_3(2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }\Delta _{\beta \gamma }+\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2) ]  
    / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }).     (5')

  
Step 6 - Final explicit formula  
Insert (3), (4') and (5') into (2).  Denoting

 A = P(\alpha ), A_1 = P'(\alpha ), B = P(\beta ), C = P(\gamma ), C_1 = P'(\gamma ),

the quartet of rational functions that constitute the coefficients of
x^4,x^3,x^2,x,1 are

 c_0 = A,

 c_1 = A_1,

 c_2 = (B - A - \Delta _{\alpha \beta }A_1) / \Delta _{\alpha \beta }^2,

 c_3 = [C - A - A_1\Delta _{\alpha \gamma } - c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2] / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }),

 c_4 = [C_1 - A_1 - 2c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma } - c_3(2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }\Delta _{\beta \gamma }+\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2)]
   / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }).                     (6)

Hence the required remainder is  

 R(x)=c_0 + c_1(x-\alpha ) + c_2(x-\alpha )^2 + c_3(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta ) + c_4(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta )(x-\gamma ).   (7)

All five coefficients are rational functions of \alpha , \beta , \gamma  and depend
linearly on the data  
P(\alpha ), P'(\alpha ), P(\beta ), P(\gamma ), P'(\gamma ), exactly as requested.  Because the highest
basis element N_4 has degree 4, R(x) is a quartic, completing the problem.

  
Verification (optional but recommended)  
A direct substitution of x=\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma  and x=\gamma  into R and R' confirms that all five
Hermite conditions are satisfied; therefore (7) is indeed the unique remainder.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher multiplicities & more nodes:  
  The divisor now has degree 5 instead of 3 and involves two double
  roots, forcing the solver to handle *five* simultaneous value/derivative
  constraints rather than the three simple ones in the original problem.

• Use of Hermite interpolation:  
  Satisfying mixed value/derivative conditions at several distinct
  points requires the Hermite–Fejér theory; elementary “plug-in and
  solve three equations” is no longer sufficient.

• Non-trivial basis construction:  
  The solver must fabricate quartic Hermite basis polynomials
  (H_{α,0}, H_{α,1}, H_{γ,0}, H_{γ,1}, L_β), check their properties,
  and manage degree considerations—considerably subtler than picking an
  arbitrary quadratic remainder.

• Derivative computations inside rational normalisations:  
  Correctly normalising by factors such as (α−β)(α−γ) and computing
  Lagrange derivatives L_i′(i) inject additional algebraic
  bookkeeping absent from the original task.

• Final expression complexity:  
  The remainder now contains fourth powers of linear factors and mixed
  products; its coefficients are rational functions with squared
  denominators, far more intricate than the simple quadratic formula of
  the kernel variant.

These added layers make the enhanced problem significantly harder,
demanding deeper theoretical insight and a longer multi-stage solution.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let F be a field of characteristic 0 and let \alpha , \beta , \gamma  \in  F be three distinct scalars.  
For an arbitrary polynomial P(x) \in  F[x] divide it by  

  D(x)= (x-\alpha )^2\cdot (x-\beta )\cdot (x-\gamma )^2.  

Write  

  P(x)=D(x) Q(x)+R(x)  with deg R \leq  4.  

1.  Prove that R(x) is uniquely determined by the five Hermite conditions  

  R(\alpha )=P(\alpha ),  R'(\alpha )=P'(\alpha ),  
  R(\beta )=P(\beta ),  
  R(\gamma )=P(\gamma ),  R'(\gamma )=P'(\gamma ).  

2.  Obtain an explicit closed-form expression for R(x) that involves only  

  P(\alpha ), P'(\alpha ), P(\beta ), P(\gamma ), P'(\gamma )  

and the parameters \alpha , \beta , \gamma .  Your final answer must be a quartic polynomial whose coefficients are rational functions of \alpha , \beta , \gamma .
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
 \Delta _{\alpha \beta }=\beta -\alpha , \Delta _{\beta \gamma }=\gamma -\beta , \Delta _{\alpha \gamma }=\gamma -\alpha .  
All three numbers are non-zero because the nodes are distinct.

  
Step 1 - Dimension count and uniqueness  
D(x) has degree 5, hence the remainder R(x) must have degree \leq  4; the F-vector
space Q_4 of such polynomials is 5-dimensional.  
The five linear functionals  

 f\mapsto f(\alpha ), f\mapsto f'(\alpha ), f\mapsto f(\beta ), f\mapsto f(\gamma ), f\mapsto f'(\gamma )  

are linearly independent on Q_4, because each of them annihilates a different
hyperplane of codimension 1.  Therefore there is a unique quartic satisfying the
required Hermite data.

  
Step 2 - A quartic Newton-Hermite basis  
Choose the (upper-triangular) Newton basis  

 N_0(x)=1,  
 N_1(x)=x-\alpha ,  
 N_2(x)=(x-\alpha )^2,  
 N_3(x)=(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta ),  
 N_4(x)=(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta )(x-\gamma ).                     (1)

deg N_i=i, so every element of Q_4 is a unique linear combination

 R(x)=c_0N_0(x)+c_1N_1(x)+c_2N_2(x)+c_3N_3(x)+c_4N_4(x).     (2)

We determine the five coefficients c_0,\ldots ,c_4 from the Hermite data.

  
Step 3 - The first three coefficients  
(i) R(\alpha )=c_0=P(\alpha ) \Rightarrow  c_0=P(\alpha ).  
(ii) R'(\alpha )=c_1=P'(\alpha ) \Rightarrow  c_1=P'(\alpha ).

(iii) R(\beta )=P(\beta ) gives  
 P(\beta )=c_0+c_1\Delta _{\alpha \beta }+c_2\Delta _{\alpha \beta }^2  
so

 c_2 = [P(\beta )-P(\alpha )-\Delta _{\alpha \beta }P'(\alpha )] / \Delta _{\alpha \beta }^2.     (3)

  
Step 4 - The fourth coefficient  
Set x=\gamma  in (2):

 P(\gamma )=P(\alpha )+P'(\alpha )\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }+c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2 + c_3\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }.     (4)

Solving for c_3 yields  

 c_3 = [P(\gamma )-P(\alpha )-P'(\alpha )\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }-c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2]  
    / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }).     (4')

  
Step 5 - The last coefficient  
Differentiate (2).  Using

 N_0'=0, N_1'=1, N_2'=2(x-\alpha ),  
 N_3'=2(x-\alpha )(x-\beta )+(x-\alpha )^2,  
 N_4'=2(x-\alpha )(x-\beta )(x-\gamma )+(x-\alpha )^2(x-\gamma )+(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta ),

evaluation at x=\gamma  gives  

 R'(\gamma )=P'(\alpha )+2c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }+c_3[2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }\Delta _{\beta \gamma }+\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2]+c_4\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }.     (5)

Because R'(\gamma )=P'(\gamma ), we solve for c_4:

 c_4 = [ P'(\gamma ) - P'(\alpha ) - 2c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma } - c_3(2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }\Delta _{\beta \gamma }+\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2) ]  
    / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }).     (5')

  
Step 6 - Final explicit formula  
Insert (3), (4') and (5') into (2).  Denoting

 A = P(\alpha ), A_1 = P'(\alpha ), B = P(\beta ), C = P(\gamma ), C_1 = P'(\gamma ),

the quartet of rational functions that constitute the coefficients of
x^4,x^3,x^2,x,1 are

 c_0 = A,

 c_1 = A_1,

 c_2 = (B - A - \Delta _{\alpha \beta }A_1) / \Delta _{\alpha \beta }^2,

 c_3 = [C - A - A_1\Delta _{\alpha \gamma } - c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2] / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }),

 c_4 = [C_1 - A_1 - 2c_2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma } - c_3(2\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }\Delta _{\beta \gamma }+\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2)]
   / (\Delta _{\alpha \gamma }^2\Delta _{\beta \gamma }).                     (6)

Hence the required remainder is  

 R(x)=c_0 + c_1(x-\alpha ) + c_2(x-\alpha )^2 + c_3(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta ) + c_4(x-\alpha )^2(x-\beta )(x-\gamma ).   (7)

All five coefficients are rational functions of \alpha , \beta , \gamma  and depend
linearly on the data  
P(\alpha ), P'(\alpha ), P(\beta ), P(\gamma ), P'(\gamma ), exactly as requested.  Because the highest
basis element N_4 has degree 4, R(x) is a quartic, completing the problem.

  
Verification (optional but recommended)  
A direct substitution of x=\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma  and x=\gamma  into R and R' confirms that all five
Hermite conditions are satisfied; therefore (7) is indeed the unique remainder.
